text: The surgical field reveals multiple implants on the uterus, left fallopian tube, and left ovary, as the surgeon prepares to perform abdoperitoneal cytology.

The surgeon performs an unilateral adnexectomy on the left side, removing the ovary and fallopian tube, with the decision on further surgical strategy pending the results of the pathological examination.

The surgeon begins the adnexectomy by cutting the fallopian tube and the utero-ovarian ligament. Following the Clermont-Ferrand technique, they open a window below the infundibulopelvic ligament, cutting the peritoneum between the round ligament and the infundibulopelvic ligament. The peritoneal section is continued to open the retroperitoneal space, where the tumor is located. The ureter is clearly visualized before beginning further dissection.

The surgeon performs ureterolysis by carefully cutting the connective tissue surrounding the right ureter, which is visible through the posterior leaf of the broad ligament. The ureter is lateralized and rotated to avoid the nearby ovarian tumor.

The surgeon carefully lateralizes the right ureter from the ovarian tumor, ensuring it is safely separated. The infundibulopelvic ligament is controlled and cut at a safe distance from the ureter. Dissection continues with the removal of peritoneum and the posterior leaf of the broad ligament, revealing visible vegetation on the peritoneum.

The surgeon uses an aspirator to lateralize the ureter, revealing the posterior leaf of the brown ligament. The left hand tracks the ovarian tumor medially, creating a visible space between the ureter and the tumor. A thin bipolar device is used to minimize thermal spread while opening the pararectal fossa, exposing the origin of the uterine artery. The surgeon attempts to circumscribe the large ovarian tumor.
The surgeon identifies a large pathological obturative node during dissection. Surrounding tissue is coagulated, and the node is carefully excised for pathological examination.

The surgeon has completely removed the node and continues the dissection anteriorly to increase the mobility of the tumor. Using coagulation, section, traction, and contra-traction, the tumor's mobility is progressively increased. The posterior leaf of the right broad ligament is visible, and the uterine structure is clearly seen.

The surgeon continues ureterolysis to lateralize and free the ureter from the pathological ovarian tumor. Small bites of tissue are coagulated to maintain a dry operative field, revealing the dissecting plane between the ureter and the ovarian tumor. The connective tissue between these structures is clearly visible as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon uses traction and contra-traction along with precise coagulation to progressively free the tumor from its attachment to the right ureter. The aspirator is used to protect the ureter laterally, revealing a clear dissection plane. Coagulated tissue is cut, and any small bleeders are controlled to maintain a clear surgical field. The residual posterior leaf of the glial ligament is cut, ensuring the ureter is safely distant from the operative field.

The surgeon identifies the uterine artery and cuts the residual part of the tumor, ensuring the ureter is safely lateralized two to three centimeters away from the operative field. The adnexectomy is completed, and the tumor is placed inside a large amber bag for extraction.

The surgeon performs a medial and infraumbilical laparotomy, closing the under back. Multiple biopsies are taken, and hemostasis is ensured. The ureter, the origin of the uterine artery, and ureteral rotation are clearly visible.

The surgeon applies a small bite of bipolar energy to control a minor bleed on the ureter. Multiple biopsies are performed on the uterine serosa, left and right paracolic areas, hepato-diaphragmatic area, and Douglas sponge. The opposite ovary is inspected, revealing a small tumor on the left residual ovary. The tumor is excised for pathological examination, while the ovary is preserved, and bipolar energy is used to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon identifies a vegetation on the greater omentum and performs an omental biopsy. Additionally, a mutation is observed on the posterior and cranial parts of the uterosacral ligament, prompting multiple biopsies. These actions are part of the strategy for addressing a suspicious ovarian tumor in the young patient.
Our next strategy will be decided with the definitive pathological examination Thank you for your attention
